claims.text: The 'Nevada Triangle,' a roughly 25,000-square-mile region of the Sierra Nevada mountains spanning Las Vegas, Fresno, and Reno, has reportedly claimed about 2,000 aircraft over the past 60 years - a per-area crash rate far exceeding the much-better-known Bermuda Triangle.

evidence.quote:

where over the past 60 years, about 50
ships and 20 planes have mysteriously crashed
or just vanished over the Atlantic.

of the Nevada triangle?
It's much smaller than the Bermuda
Triangle and much more dangerous.

triangle has claimed two thousand aircraft.
And we really watch that
almost one plane a week.

crashes in the Nevada triangle that's so strange.

really talks about it.

the Sierra Nevada mountains between Nevada and
California, some 2000 planes have

claims.rationale: There really is a high concentration of aircraft wrecks in the central Sierra Nevada - the Civil Air Patrol catalog lists hundreds of crash sites in this corridor. The 2,000 figure is a popular Discovery Channel / 'Nevada Triangle' documentary number; rigorous tallies usually come in lower (a few hundred to ~1,000 over 60 years). The geographic framing as a 'triangle' bracketing the major desert airports is correct.

claims.text: An unusual share of Nevada Triangle disappearances involve highly experienced pilots flying state-of-the-art military aircraft, not weekend hobbyists - which makes simple pilot error a less satisfying explanation than terrain or weather alone.
entities: technology:state of the art military aircraft

in cross-country skiing, mountain climbing.
He did ultramarathons, a triathlete.

survive anything, it was Steve Fossett

claims.rationale: It is true that the area is heavily used for military training (Naval Air Station Fallon, Edwards/China Lake transit, etc.) and that several high-profile crashes have involved military jets and credentialed civil aviators. The framing that pilot skill should make crashes 'unexplained' actually understates how unforgiving Sierra mountain weather is to even very experienced pilots.

claims.text: World-record aviator Steve Fossett vanished on September 3, 2007 over Nevada in a single-engine Bellanca; despite a month-long search he was declared dead, and only the following year did a hiker find his ID and a crash site about 65 miles from his takeoff point, with two confirmed bone fragments recovered nearby.

claims.rationale: Fossett's September 3, 2007 disappearance is one of the most-covered aviation stories of the 2000s. The hiker discovery of his ID in October 2008, the subsequent crash site location in the Sierras east of Mammoth Lakes, and DNA-confirmed bone fragments are all in the official NTSB report and contemporaneous news coverage.

claims.text: A 1943 B-24 bomber crash in the Sierras illustrates the region's pattern: the original aircraft vanished on a training flight from Hammer Field, a search-and-rescue B-24 sent the next day also disappeared, and the second wreck wasn't found until Huntington Lake Reservoir was drained for dam repairs in 1955.

claims.rationale: The 1943 B-24 disappearance from Hammer Field (often misspelled in transcripts as 'Whammer'), the lost search-bomber follow-up, and the 1955 Huntington Lake Reservoir drainage discovery are all documented in Army Air Forces and Forest Service records. Wind turbulence and downdrafts off the Sierra crest are the standard official explanation.

claims.text: Air Force Lieutenant David Steeves vanished over the Sierras in May 1957 in a T-33 trainer, was declared dead, then walked out of the wilderness 54 days later claiming he had survived after ejecting and crawling 20 miles to an abandoned cabin - a story dismissed as Cold War defection until Boy Scouts found his ejected canopy 20 years later.

evidence.quote:
Another crazy story happened in 1957.

The plane disappeared after a
thorough search without success.
The Air Force declared the twenty

gaunt and dressed in tattered clothing.
He had made his way to a camp in the
back country of Kings Canyon National Park, east of
Fresno. He said something in the plane exploded,
so he ejected and dragging his parachute
to keep warm and injured from his landing.
He crawled over twenty miles in freezing
temperatures for fifteen days without food or
shelter. Eventually, he came upon an abandoned cabin
where he found a few cans of food
and fishing gear. He said he survived
by fishing and hunting with his pistol.

claims.rationale: The David Steeves story is a documented USAF case from May 9, 1957: T-33 explosion, 54-day survival walk-out from Kings Canyon, public disbelief and accusations of selling the jet to the Soviets, and the 1977 Boy Scouts canopy find that vindicated him posthumously. The host gets the aircraft type slightly wrong ('T-3' should be T-33).

claims.text: On July 11, 1986, Major Ross Mulhare flew a then-still-classified F-117 Nighthawk into a Sierra mountain near Bakersfield; congressional sources later said he was flying an experimental aircraft with classified materials and structural features, but the official cause has never been publicly confirmed.

triang

claims.rationale: Major Ross Mulhare did die crashing an F-117A in the Sequoia National Forest on July 11, 1986. The aircraft type was still classified at the time (the F-117's existence was not officially acknowledged until November 1988), so the immediate official cause was withheld. Subsequent reporting attributes the crash to pilot fatigue / spatial disorientation, not exotic structural features.

claims.text: The mainstream meteorological explanation for the Nevada Triangle is the 'mountain wave' phenomenon: the Sierra Nevada crest sits perpendicular to the jet stream and generates violent downdrafts capable of overpowering an aircraft's climb rate, as appears to have happened to Steve Fossett's plane.

claims.rationale: Mountain-wave turbulence on the lee side of the Sierras is a well-known and well-documented hazard in aviation meteorology - the FAA, NOAA, and AOPA all describe it. Sierra wave downdrafts of 600-1500+ feet per minute have been measured, easily exceeding the climb rate of light aircraft. The NTSB's Fossett report listed downdrafts as a probable factor.

claims.text: In the host's verdict, the conspiracy explanation - that secret Air Force prototype testing out of Area 51 is responsible for the Nevada Triangle disappearances - is less well supported than the prosaic combination of mountain-wave weather, rugged terrain, and dense vegetation that conceals crash sites for decades.

evidence.quote:

volatile, unpredictable winds and downdrafts.
This weather phenomenon is sometimes called the
mountain wave, where planes are seemingly
ripped from the air and
crashed to the ground.

and the heavy vegetation.

claims.rationale: An accurate paraphrase of the host's verdict, which is itself well-calibrated. The 'eight other crash sites found while searching for Fossett' detail is real - it became famous because it shows the back-country is genuinely littered with undiscovered wrecks, supporting the prosaic explanation over the Area 51 conspiracy.
